1: A fresh oracle-introduction: wayĕhî dĕbar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr — the standard prophetic formula for a new divine word. The MKT follows MT in marking this as a new unit. The commands that follow (celibacy, no mourning, no feasting) are sign-acts, not merely moral instructions.
2: lōʾ-tiqqaḥ lĕkā ʾiššâ — "You must not marry or have sons or daughters in this place"; celibacy as prophetic sign-act — one of the most unusual divine commands given to an OT prophet. The MKT renders "in this place" (bammāqôm hazzeh) as specifying Jerusalem — marriage and family in a place destined for destruction would be an act of false hope. The celibacy enacts the end of normal life.
3: The reason for the marriage-prohibition stated explicitly: sons and daughters born in this place will die of terrible diseases (bĕmōtê taḥalûʾîm). Their mothers and fathers will also die without the covenant dignities of mourning and burial. The MKT follows MT in listing the deaths of parents alongside children — the sign-act encompasses whole family systems.
4: bĕmōtê taḥalûʾîm — "diseases" or "terrible diseases"; taḥalûʾ means a sickness or wasting disease. "Like dung on the surface of the ground" (kĕdōmen ʿal-pĕnê hāʾădāmâ) — the covenant curse of unburied dead (Deuteronomy 28:26). The MKT preserves the dignity-stripping directness: no mourning, no burial, just corpses as manure.
5: bêt marzēaḥ — "house of mourning"; marzēaḥ was a formal mourning institution or feast associated with death in the ancient Near East. YHWH withdraws his šālôm (well-being), ḥesed (covenant loyalty), and raḥămîm (compassion) from this generation. The MKT renders all three terms because each represents a different covenantal quality being suspended.
6: The prohibition on the normal mourning rites: no cutting (lōʾ-yissĕrĕṭû) and no shaving one's head (lōʾ-yiqqārĕḥû) for the dead. These were standard mourning gestures (though the Torah also restricted them, Leviticus 19:28; Jeremiah 21:5). The MKT follows MT in specifying both rites — the complete social apparatus of mourning is suspended.
7: lōʾ-yiprĕsû lāhem leḥem ʿal-ʾēbel — "No one will offer food to the mourner to comfort him for the dead"; the breaking of bread with mourners was the social solidarity ritual. "The cup of consolation" (kôs tanaḥûmîm) was a ceremonial cup given to the bereaved. The suspension of both communal mourning customs signals the breakdown of society's capacity for mutual comfort.
8: The second prohibition: bêt mišteh (house of feasting) is also off-limits. Where Jeremiah 16:5 forbids mourning and Jeremiah 16:8 forbids feasting, Jeremiah's entire social existence — from grief to celebration — is withdrawn. This total social isolation enacts the coming desolation when both mourning and feasting will be silenced (see Jeremiah 7:34).
9: mašbît mizzeh-hammāqôm lĕʿênêkem ûbîmêkem — "I am about to silence in this place — within your own sight and in your own days"; YHWH will silence qôl śāśôn wĕqôl śimḥâ qôl ḥātān wĕqôl kallâ — the bridal-joy four-phrase formula (cf. Jeremiah 7:34; Jeremiah 25:10). The temporal specification "within your own sight and in your own days" means those alive now will witness this.
10: The people's anticipated question: why has YHWH declared all this disaster? The question uses the covenant-word rāʿâ (evil, disaster) — the people recognize the announcement as disaster but not yet as their fault. The MKT preserves the naive question ("What wrong have we done?") because it sets up the specific answer in Jeremiah 16:11-13.
11: taḥat ʾăšer-ʿāzĕbû ʾôtî ʾăbôtêkem — "Because your ancestors forsook me"; the generational dimension of apostasy — the ancestors' rejection of YHWH. The fourfold description of their idolatry: went after (hālĕkû ʾaḥărê), served (wayyaʿăbdûm), worshipped (wayyištaḥăwû lāhem), forsook (wĕʾōtî ʿāzābû) — four verbs of increasing commitment to idolatry.
12: waʾattem hăraʿōtem laʿăśôt — "you have done even worse than your ancestors" — the downward spiral of apostasy. "Every one of you follows the stubborn evil of his own heart" (ʾîš ʾaḥărê šĕrîrût libbô hārāʿ): šĕrîrût lēb (stubbornness of heart) is Jeremiah's diagnostic phrase. The refusal to "listen to me" (lĕbiltî šĕmōaʿ ʾēlay) — the covenant obligation of hearing-obedience abandoned.
13: wĕhêtaltî ʾetkem mēʿal hāʾādāmâ — "I will cast you out of this land"; hētaltî (hurl, fling) conveys a violent expulsion. "Into a land that neither you nor your ancestors have ever known" — the reversal of the covenant land-gift. "There you may serve other gods day and night" — the bitterly ironic permission: you wanted idols, you can have them in exile without YHWH.
14: ʾim-lōʾ hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm — "But days are coming"; the transition from judgment to restoration oracle. The new exodus will supersede the Exodus formula: "as the LORD lives who brought Israel out of Egypt" will be replaced by a new oath formula. The MKT's "people will no longer say: As the LORD lives who brought Israel out of Egypt" preserves the surprising eclipse of the foundational confession.
15: ʾim-kî ḥay-YHWH ʾăšer heʿĕlâ ʾet-bĕnê Yiśrāʾēl mēʾerets ṣāpôn — "As the LORD lives who brought Israel back from the land of the north"; the second exodus will define YHWH more than the Egyptian exodus. "From all the countries where he had driven them" (mikkol hāʾărāṣôt ʾăšer hiddîḥam šāmmâ) — the global scatter reversed by a global gather. The MKT preserves the geographical specificity: north and all countries.
16: hinnĕnî šōlēaḥ lĕdayyāgîm rabbîm — "I am sending for many fishermen" — YHWH as the one who sends agents of judgment. The fishermen and hunters are metaphors for the Babylonian armies and their systematic capture of the exiles. The imagery moves from fishing to hunting: both are thorough — no one escapes a comprehensive catch.
17: kî ʿênay ʿal-kol-darkêhem — "For my eyes are on all their ways"; the divine omniscience provides the warrant for comprehensive judgment. Lōʾ nistarû minnĕged pānāy — "they are not hidden from my sight" — the absolute transparency of human action before YHWH. Wĕlōʾ niṣpan ʿăwōnām minnĕged ʿênāy — even their guilt (ʿāwōn) is not hidden.
18: ûšillāmtî rīšōnâ mišneh ʿăwōnām wĕḥaṭṭāʾtām — "First I will repay their guilt and their sin in double measure"; mišneh (double) — the full covenant-justice repayment. "Because they have defiled my land with the lifeless images of their detestable idols" (nigbĕlātêhem) — the land-pollution language frames idolatry as a pollution crime against the covenantal territory.
19: YHWH ʿuzzî ûmāʿuzzî ûmānôsî bĕyôm ṣārâ — "O LORD, my strength and my fortified refuge in the day of distress"; Jeremiah's prayer of confidence framed as a future hope. The nations coming from the ends of the earth (ʾaprĕsê-ʾārets) is a vision of universal recognition of YHWH's supremacy. Their confession: "our ancestors inherited nothing but falsehood" (lōʾ-mōʿîl) — the idols are exposed as useless.
20: hăyaʿăśeh-lô ʾādām ʾĕlōhîm — "Can a person make gods for himself?" The rhetorical question exposes the absurdity of idol-making. "But they are no gods at all" (wĕhēmmâ lōʾ ʾĕlōhîm) — the simple declaration. The surrounding nations' confession (Jeremiah 16:19-20) makes this statement ecumenically significant: even Gentiles will recognize the idols' non-existence.
21: hinnĕnî môdîʿām bappaʿam hazzōʾt — "I am going to make them know — this time I will make them know my hand and my strength"; the double yādaʿ (know) — two different qal forms for knowing by experience. Yādĕʿû ʾet-šĕmî YHWH — they will know my name is YHWH. The formula "that I am the LORD" (the recognition formula) is the goal of the entire judgment sequence.
